original_abstract:
This paper introduces the Adaptive Recursive Neural Network (AdaRNN), a novel deep learning framework designed for target-dependent sentiment classification on Twitter. Unlike traditional sentiment analysis models that treat a tweet as a single unit, AdaRNN focuses on identifying the specific sentiment directed toward a particular entity within a post, which may contain multiple targets and conflicting opinions.

To achieve this, the model utilizes dependency parsing to restructure tweets into a recursive format where the target entity serves as the root. The core innovation of AdaRNN is the use of an adaptive multi-compositionality layer. Rather than relying on a single static weight matrix for semantic composition, the model employs multiple composition functions and learns a distribution to select the most appropriate one based on context and syntactic relationships. This allows the network to dynamically propagate sentiment from individual words to the target while effectively handling complex linguistic phenomena like negation and intensification.

In addition to the architectural contributions, the authors provide a new, manually annotated dataset for target-dependent Twitter sentiment analysis. Experimental results demonstrate that AdaRNN significantly outperforms standard recursive neural networks and baseline SVM methods that rely on hand-crafted linguistic rules, highlighting the advantages of learning adaptive sentiment representations from data.
humanized_abstract:
In this paper, we propose the Adaptive Recursive Neural Network (AdaRNN), a deep learning model that is designed for the task of target-dependent sentiment classification on Twitter. While existing work treats a tweet as a whole sample for sentiment analysis, the task of target-dependent sentiment classification considers a tweet as a sequence of opinions towards different targets, and hence requires the model to focus on a specific target and output a sentiment label corresponding to that target. Most of the time, a tweet can contain multiple targets and expression of conflicting opinions.

For this task, we train a model that takes the tweets and transforms them into a dependency parse tree, that is recursively projected to a tree where the entities of interest are at the root. Our model, AdaRNN, uses an adaptive multi-compositionality layer. In contrast to traditional approaches that use a fixed weight matrix for the semantic composition, the ideas here is to have multiple composition functions and learn the distribution over the composition functions to choose the optimal function for the particular context and syntax of the input and thus allow the network to appropriately diffuse the word-level sentiment to the root entity, while at the same time, enabling the model to manage the different challenges presented by diverse linguistic patterns in the tweets, including negations and intensifiers.

The authors draw on their knowledge of architecture in proposing a solution to the problem of modeling tweet sentiments, and also introduce a new manually annotated dataset of a large scale of examples to the existing literature related to the target-dependent Twitter sentiment analysis. They prove in their experimentations that their proposed approach AdaRNN (Adaptive Recurrent Neural Network) shows significantly better performances than the traditional recursive neural networks as well as the popular SVM approach based on human expertise that depends on selecting relevant features from linguistic patterns in the tweets.

document.output:
In this paper, we propose the Adaptive Recursive Neural Network (AdaRNN), a deep learning model that is designed for the task of target-dependent sentiment classification on Twitter. While existing work treats a tweet as a whole sample for sentiment analysis, the task of target-dependent sentiment classification considers a tweet as a sequence of opinions towards different targets, and hence requires the model to focus on a specific target and output a sentiment label corresponding to that target. Most of the time, a tweet can contain multiple targets and expression of conflicting opinions.

For this task, we train a model that takes the tweets and transforms them into a dependency parse tree, that is recursively projected to a tree where the entities of interest are at the root. Our model, AdaRNN, uses an adaptive multi-compositionality layer. In contrast to traditional approaches that use a fixed weight matrix for the semantic composition, the ideas here is to have multiple composition functions and learn the distribution over the composition functions to choose the optimal function for the particular context and syntax of the input and thus allow the network to appropriately diffuse the word-level sentiment to the root entity, while at the same time, enabling the model to manage the different challenges presented by diverse linguistic patterns in the tweets, including negations and intensifiers.

The authors draw on their knowledge of architecture in proposing a solution to the problem of modeling tweet sentiments, and also introduce a new manually annotated dataset of a large scale of examples to the existing literature related to the target-dependent Twitter sentiment analysis. They prove in their experimentations that their proposed approach AdaRNN (Adaptive Recurrent Neural Network) shows significantly better performances than the traditional recursive neural networks as well as the popular SVM approach based on human expertise that depends on selecting relevant features from linguistic patterns in the tweets.
document.input:
This paper introduces the Adaptive Recursive Neural Network (AdaRNN), a novel deep learning framework designed for target-dependent sentiment classification on Twitter. Unlike traditional sentiment analysis models that treat a tweet as a single unit, AdaRNN focuses on identifying the specific sentiment directed toward a particular entity within a post, which may contain multiple targets and conflicting opinions.

To achieve this, the model utilizes dependency parsing to restructure tweets into a recursive format where the target entity serves as the root. The core innovation of AdaRNN is the use of an adaptive multi-compositionality layer. Rather than relying on a single static weight matrix for semantic composition, the model employs multiple composition functions and learns a distribution to select the most appropriate one based on context and syntactic relationships. This allows the network to dynamically propagate sentiment from individual words to the target while effectively handling complex linguistic phenomena like negation and intensification.

In addition to the architectural contributions, the authors provide a new, manually annotated dataset for target-dependent Twitter sentiment analysis. Experimental results demonstrate that AdaRNN significantly outperforms standard recursive neural networks and baseline SVM methods that rely on hand-crafted linguistic rules, highlighting the advantages of learning adaptive sentiment representations from data.
